headline: Bobby Witt Jr. Goes First Overall, Ohtani Falls to Pick 2, and Somebody Named "Buttville Skidmarks" Might Actually Win This Thing
subheadline: The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft is in the books, and we have steals, reaches, dynasties, and at least one team that drafted like they were asleep at the keyboard.
body:
## **I. The War Room Opens**

You know that scene in *The Dark Knight* where the Joker walks into the mob meeting and everyone's simultaneously terrified and fascinated and nobody quite knows what's about to happen? That's every fantasy baseball draft. Fourteen managers, months of prep, spreadsheets that would make a NASA engineer weep, and somehow — *somehow* — someone still drafts a guy who retired in February. The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft went down on March 22nd, and I've watched every pick, rewound the tape, cross-referenced the ADP data, and consumed an irresponsible amount of the league's title sponsor in the process. What I can tell you is this: there were moments of genuine brilliance, moments of what-are-you-doing energy, and at least one team building something that looks genuinely terrifying going into the season.

The headline steals are real — we're talking players landing two, three rounds below their consensus value, the kind of picks that win championships in October while the guy who reached on a 72-wRC+ bat is posting angry messages in the group chat. The biggest villain of the day? A team that has historically finished in the basement and somehow still found ways to confuse "bold strategy" with "panicking at the keyboard." The biggest hero? A squad that just won the whole thing last year and drafted like they knew exactly what they were doing, which — spoiler — they did. This is your definitive 2026 BeerLeagueBaseball draft review. Let's get into it.

---

## **II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

**North Correa** held the first overall pick and did exactly what you'd expect from a team that finished 14th and then 12th in back-to-back seasons: they took the safest, most consensus-friendly player available. **Bobby Witt Jr.** goes first overall, and honestly, it's hard to argue. The man is a five-category monster — runs, homers, RBI, steals, and a batting average that doesn't embarrass you. In a 12-category league that includes SB and H/AB, Witt Jr. is a near-perfect anchor. Is he the most *exciting* pick at one? Maybe not. But when you've finished last twice, you don't get cute. You take the sure thing and figure out the rest.

From there, Round 1 unfolded with the kind of logic you'd expect — **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** to **Athletic Park Dragons** at two (the reigning 14th-place finisher doing their best impression of a team that's been paying attention), **Mookie Betts** to **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** at three, **Aaron Judge** to **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** at four (the defending-adjacent contender staying the course), and **Elly De La Cruz** to **BaseballPro77** at five. The back half of the first round is where it got interesting: **José Ramírez** at six to **Albuquerque Bombers** (perennially good, never quite winning), **Juan Soto** at seven to **Hiroshima Radiation**, **Vladdy Guerrero Jr.** at eight to **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** (the perennial nearly-team), **Kyle Tucker** at nine to **Buttville Skidmarks** (yes, really), **Francisco Lindor** at ten to **General Hospital** (two straight 13th-place finishes, so sure), **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at eleven to **Sugar Land Skeeters** (the reigning champs, picking eleventh like a quarterback handed a sixth-round slot because they're *that* good), **Jackson Chourio** at twelve to **Riverside Roughnecks**, **Corbin Carroll** at thirteen to **Canberra Wombats**, and **Julio Rodríguez** at fourteen to **Benchwarmers**. No disasters at the top. No surprises that made you spit out your drink. But buckle up — the middle rounds are where the real story lives.

---

## **III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa** — *Pick #1*
Two consecutive cellar finishes (14th, 12th) and now the first overall pick. The pressure was enormous and **Bobby Witt Jr.** was the right call — elite in steals, power, and run-scoring in a format that rewards exactly those categories. Round 2 brought **Pete Alonso** (Baltimore Orioles), which is fine — power bat, serviceable RBI machine, but the move to Baltimore adds a layer of uncertainty. **Logan Gilbert** in Round 3 is a legitimate SP2 with solid strikeout numbers and WHIP. The concern here is the middle of the draft: **Sandy Alcantara** (Round 8) is a massive injury gamble, and **Spencer Strider** (Round 9) is another guy coming off a torn UCL. North Correa is essentially betting on two pitchers to stay healthy who have recent catastrophic injury histories. That's either bold or reckless, and history suggests it's the latter for a team that can't afford a miss. The floor here is high, the ceiling is dependent on the medical reports.
**Grade: B-** — *Great anchor, but the pitching staff reads like an ER waiting room.*

---

**Athletic Park Dragons** — *Pick #2*
Finished 6th then 14th — the classic "we were good, then we weren't" arc. **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** at two is automatic. Elite OBP, elite power, elite everything — at Util, he fills a roster spot efficiently in a 14-team league. **Matt Olson** in Round 2 is a legitimate power bat, and building out the rotation with **Michael King**, **Shota Imanaga**, and **Luis Castillo** in rounds 4 through 7 gives this team real pitching depth. The concern is the offense outside Ohtani and Olson — **Kyle Schwarber** is fine but one-dimensional, **Ezequiel Tovar** is a Colorado Rockies shortstop (the fantasy graveyard), and **Nick Castellanos** in Round 11 feels like 2021 nostalgia shopping. If the pitching staff stays healthy, this is a top-six team. If it doesn't, they're looking at a third straight disappointing finish.
**Grade: B** — *Ohtani anchors everything; the pitching depth is real but the offense gets thin fast.*

---

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** — *Pick #3*
Finished 10th last year. **Mookie Betts** at three is elite — multi-position eligibility in a 12-category format is gold, and his 2025 numbers remind everyone he's still one of the five best fantasy players alive. **Ketel Marte** in Round 2 is a steal-adjacent pick with 2B eligibility and consistent production. The problem? This team loaded up on relievers late — **Justin Martinez**, **Jeff Hoffman**, **Jason Foley**, **Jason Adam** — which is either a brilliant NSVH/ERA streaming strategy or a bunch of guys who will be unavailable in two weeks. **William Contreras** at catcher in Round 3 is excellent. **Christian Yelich** in Round 6 is the "I remember what he was in 2018" pick, and it's fine as a late-round flier, but he's not that guy anymore. A functional squad that could make noise in the middle of the standings.
**Grade: B-** — *Solid top-end talent, but the late rounds are a reliever lottery ticket.*

---

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins** — *Pick #4*
One championship, consistent top-ten finishes — this is a well-run operation. **Aaron Judge** at four is the pick, and Judge is Judge: 50-plus homers, elite OBP, and he anchors the OF/HR/RBI categories like a freight train. **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** in Round 2 adds the speed and positional versatility you need for a championship roster. **Austin Riley** in Round 3 is a rock-solid 3B. The rotation built around **Pablo López**, **Spencer Schwellenbach**, and **Zac Gallen** is real — not flashy, but the kind of consistent depth that wins 12-category leagues. **Brice Turang** and **Xavier Edwards** in rounds 8-9 give you SB upside that other teams will envy come August. This team looks like what it always looks like: competent, deep, dangerous.
**Grade: A-** — *Championship pedigree plus a draft that screamed "we know what we're doing."*

---

**BaseballPro77** — *Pick #5*
Two-time champion, finished 7th last year — they're either reloading or declining, and this draft suggests reloading. **Elly De La Cruz** at five is the upside play of the first round — if the strikeouts tick down and the steals stay elite (he stole 67 bases in 2024, folks), this is a top-three pick. **Jackson Merrill** in Round 2 is a genuine steal — breakout OF with real production last year. **Emmanuel Clase** in Round 4 gives you the saves anchor every contender needs. **Cole Ragans** in Round 3 has ace-adjacent stuff in Kansas City. The back end gets creative: **Hunter Greene** in Round 9 is a high-strikeout boom-or-bust arm, and **Luis Arraez** in Round 10 is the batting average floor every roster needs. Former champions draft with purpose. This reads like a team that wants to win again.
**Grade: A** — *Two championships in three years, and this roster has the pieces to make it three.*

---

**Albuquerque Bombers** — *Pick #6*
Third place last year, never won — the eternal bridesmaid. **José Ramírez** at six is perfect: he's a perennial top-five fantasy player with 3B eligibility, and in a category-scoring league, his multi-category contributions are unmatched. **Freddie Freeman** in Round 2 and **Garrett Crochet** in Round 3 are both excellent. Crochet, in particular, is a potential Cy Young candidate with Boston. **Logan Webb** in Round 5 is a WHIP monster. **Willy Adames** and **Xander Bogaerts** give you middle-infield depth. **Hunter Brown** and **Justin Steele** add rotation depth without breaking the bank. The Bombers look genuinely dangerous this year — maybe the year the bridesmaid finally catches the bouquet.
**Grade: A-** — *Loaded roster top to bottom; José Ramírez at six might be the best value pick of Round 1.*

---

**Hiroshima Radiation** — *Pick #7*
Finished 2nd, 11th, 5th — the definition of inconsistency. **Juan Soto** at seven is elite — his OBP alone (.419 in 2025) is a category weapon in a 12-cat format. **Trea Turner** in Round 2 adds speed you need. The rotation is where this gets messy: **Blake Snell** in Round 3 is the highest-variance SP in baseball (pure ace when healthy, vanishes constantly), and **Tyler Glasnow** in Round 5 has similar health concerns. Hiroshima is essentially building a pitching staff out of "what if they stay healthy this time?" energy. **Robbie Ray** in Round 10 after Tommy John is another one. Look, if all these arms stay healthy, this is a top-three team. The last two times Hiroshima had a great roster it finished 2nd and 5th. Make of that what you will.
**Grade: B** — *Soto and Turner are elite, but this rotation is a house of health-risk cards.*

---

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — *Pick #8*
Finished 4th, 5th, 2nd — perpetually knocking on the door. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at eight is excellent value in a run-scoring format. **Bryce Harper** in Round 2 adds a second elite bat. **Corbin Burnes** in Round 3 is a legitimate SP1. **Framber Valdez** in Round 4 gives you an innings-eating WHIP machine. **Ronald Acuña Jr.** in Round 5 — look, if he comes back healthy from his ACL tear, this is potentially the steal of the entire draft. If not, it's a wasted pick. The Wonderdogs are built to finally break through and actually win the thing. The roster is too good to finish second again. Right?
**Grade: A-** — *If Acuña is healthy, this might be the best team in the league. That's a big "if."*

---

**Buttville Skidmarks** — *Pick #9*
Finished 11th, 8th, 8th — slowly improving, never threatening. **Kyle Tucker** at nine is an excellent pick — elite OF with power, average, and category depth across the board. **Zack Wheeler** in Round 2 is a legitimate ace. **Rafael Devers** in Round 3 gives you power at 3B. **Corey Seager** in Round 4 — when healthy — is elite. **Jacob deGrom** in Round 5 is the ballsiest pick of the draft. DeGrom hasn't thrown meaningful innings since before some of these managers' kids were born. But if he comes back? Absolute ace. The Skidmarks are gambling on health at every turn — deGrom, Seager's hamstrings, Devers adjusting to San Francisco — and somehow it feels like it might work? This is the most fascinating roster in the league.
**Grade: B+** — *Swing-for-the-fences roster with legitimate upside; health is the only question and it's a big one.*

---

**General Hospital** — *Pick #10*
Back-to-back 13th-place finishes. The name is becoming prophecy. **Francisco Lindor** at ten is an excellent pick — elite SS with multi-category production and Mets lineup protection. **Jarren Duran** in Round 2 is a genuine steal — he emerged as a legitimate fantasy asset in 2025. **Chris Sale** in Round 3 is vintage Hospital: a pitcher who was great once and might be again. **Mike Trout** in Round 6 is the most heartbreaking pick in the draft — a former all-time great who hasn't played a full season since the Obama administration. The roster construction reads like someone who's watched every *Field of Dreams* re-run and believes in ghosts. Lindor and Duran give you something to work with, but Trout and Sale are prayers more than picks.
**Grade: C+** — *Two promising picks buried under too much "remember when this guy was great?" energy.*

---

**Sugar Land Skeeters** — *Pick #11*
Reigning champions. Picking eleventh. Absolutely unfazed. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at eleven is possibly the best value pick in Round 1 — a player with genuine top-five upside sliding to eleven while teams at the top took safer options. **Tarik Skubal** in Round 2 might be the best pitcher in baseball right now. **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** in Round 4 gives you a second ace-caliber arm. **Ryan Helsley** in Round 7 anchors saves. The Skeeters took the Spurs approach — drafted calmly, got elite talent at each slot, and built a roster that looks terrifyingly good. **Cody Bellinger** in Round 3 is the wild card — when he's on, he's genuinely excellent; when he's not, he's very not. But with Tatis and Skubal as your foundation? You can afford one gamble.
**Grade: A** — *Champions drafting like champions; Tatis at 11 might win them a second straight title.*

---

**Riverside Roughnecks** — *Pick #12*
Finished 9th, 9th, 11th — trending the wrong direction. **Jackson Chourio** at twelve is legitimate upside — 25-plus homers and speed from a 21-year-old. **Gunnar Henderson** in Round 2 is an excellent pick, elite SS. **Ozzie Albies** in Round 3 gives you 2B production. **Mason Miller** in Round 6 is a legitimate saves anchor. **Roki Sasaki** in Round 7 might be the most interesting SP pick in the draft — the Dodger machine will elevate his numbers. **James Wood** in Round 5 adds another young OF piece with upside. This team is quietly well-constructed with a youth movement that could surprise people. The trajectory has been down; the draft suggests a correction.
**Grade: B+** — *Young, fast, and underrated — the sleeper team of the 2026 draft.*

---

**Canberra Wombats** — *Pick #13*
Finished 3rd, 12th, 4th — classic yo-yo team. **Corbin Carroll** at thirteen is fine but possibly a slight reach given his 2025 regression. The real score? **Yordan Alvarez** in Round 2 is a monster pick — elite power, elite OBP, and he immediately becomes this team's offensive anchor. **Wyatt Langford** in Round 3 adds OF upside. **Josh Hader** in Round 5 gives you one of the best closers in the game. **Cal Raleigh** in Round 6 at catcher is excellent value. **Shane McClanahan** in Round 13 is a flier worth taking on a pitcher with his ceiling. The Wombats draft better than they finish, and this might finally be the year those two things align.
**Grade: B+** — *Alvarez in Round 2 is highway robbery; this team is better than their recent history suggests.*

---

**Benchwarmers** — *Pick #14*
Finished 14th, 7th, 9th — a team still finding itself. **Julio Rodríguez** at fourteen is fair value — elite OF with speed and power upside. But the real story? **Paul Skenes** in Round 2 at Pick 15 is *the* steal of the entire draft — the most exciting young arm in baseball, Cy Young candidate, and he slides to 15? Criminal. **Oneil Cruz** in Round 3 adds speed and power. **Dylan Cease** in Round 4 gives you strikeout upside. **Edwin Díaz** in Round 5 anchors saves. **Adley Rutschman** in Round 6 is one of the best catchers in baseball. The Benchwarmers, picking last in Round 1, might have pulled off the best draft in the room.
**Grade: A-** — *Paul Skenes at 15 is the pick of the draft; this team is a dark horse contender.*

---

## **IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**The Three Best Steals:**

1. **Paul Skenes, Round 2, Pick 15, Benchwarmers** — The reigning NL Rookie of the Year frontrunner and the most electric young pitcher in baseball falling to the 15th pick overall is genuinely baffling. This is a legitimate SP1 going in Round 2. Robbery. Call the cops.

2. **Yordan Alvarez, Round 2, Pick 16, Canberra Wombats** — Elite power, elite OBP, perennial top-ten fantasy hitter. Picking this late in Round 2 in a 14-team league is a gift. The Wombats should send a thank-you card to every team that passed.

3. **Tarik Skubal, Round 2, Pick 18, Sugar Land Skeeters** — The reigning AL Cy Young winner going 18th overall? The reigning champions just got better. Skubal in 2025 had a 2.99 ERA and 228 strikeouts. At Pick 18, that's larceny.

**The Three Worst Reaches:**

1. **Mike Trout, Round 6, Pick 75, General Hospital** — Trout's last fully healthy season might predate some of these managers' marriages. At Round 6, you're burning a pick on a prayer. The Hospital is living up to its name.

2. **Corbin Carroll, Round 1, Pick 13, Canberra Wombats** — Carroll regressed significantly in 2025 after his breakout, and taking him at 13th overall when the Wombats then scored Alvarez at 16 suggests they could have waited. The first-round price was steep for a bounce-back project.

3. **Sandy Alcantara + Spencer Strider back-to-back, Rounds 8-9, North Correa** — Taking one pitcher coming off major arm surgery is a gamble. Taking two consecutive pitchers coming off major arm surgery is a *manifesto*. North Correa is either brave or delusional, and the standings will tell us which.

---

## **V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

1. **Buttville Skidmarks finish in the top three.** Jacob deGrom returns to form, Kyle Tucker anchors the OF, and this team — finally — stops being the "almost" squad and becomes a legitimate contender. The name stays ridiculous. The roster does not.

2. **General Hospital finishes last for the third consecutive year.** Mike Trout doesn't play 100 games, Chris Sale misses two months, and Francisco Lindor can't carry the entire roster by himself. The name was a prophecy all along.

3. **Paul Skenes wins the MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Pitcher of the Week award more than any other arm in the league.** The Benchwarmers got a generational talent at Pick 15. He's going to remind everyone why every week.

4. **Albuquerque Bombers finally break through and win their first championship.** José Ramírez, Freddie Freeman, Garrett Crochet — this is their best roster yet, and the drafting was too disciplined for lightning to not strike. The eternal bridesmaid gets her moment.

5. **The Sugar Land Skeeters become back-to-back champions**, making them the first repeat winner in BeerLeagueBaseball history. Tatis at 11, Skubal at 18, a balanced roster from top to bottom — they drafted like a team that *knew* they were better than everyone else in the room. Because they were.

---

## **VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

My pick is **Sugar Land Skeeters**.

Here's why: championships aren't won at Pick 1. They're won by teams who extract maximum value from every slot they're given, and the Skeeters — picking 11th in Round 1 — walked out of this draft with **Fernando Tatis Jr.**, **Tarik Skubal**, **Cody Bellinger**, **Yoshinobu Yamamoto**, and **Ryan Helsley**. They have an OF that can hit, an SP staff that can dominate, and a closer who gets saves. They won it all last year finishing first, and they drafted this year like a team that has zero intention of giving the trophy back.

The Skeeters are the Golden State Warriors circa 2017 — already champions, already dangerous, and somehow just got *better* at the draft. Everyone in this league should be mildly terrified, and the MillerLite® should be pre-chilled for October.

*The first overall pick was Bobby Witt Jr. The last overall pick is going to be the Sugar Land Skeeters — last to lose it, that is.*
